article_title: Pure ventriculoscopic resection of an intraventricular meningioma with a basket retriever through a single burr hole: illustrative case
label: FIG. 2.
caption: NSB-assisted ETV and removal of the intraventricular tumor. A: Expanded tip of the NSB. B: The floor of the third ventricle was penetrated with the blunt tip of the NSB. C: The stoma of the third ventricle was expanded by deploying NSB. D: A small, round lesion with a narrow stalk was located in the trigone of the left lateral ventricle. E: Initial attempts to grasp the tumor and control its stalk using standard endoscopic instruments were unsuccessful, as the lesion swung away with each maneuver. After several attempts, small bleeding occurred. F: A microbipolar forceps were used to coagulate the stalk. G: The NSB was then inserted through the endoscope’s irrigation channel and deployed to trap and fix the lesion. H: Once the basket securely encaged the tumor, ventriculoscopic scissors were used to transect the stalk of the lesion. I: The tumor was held with the NSB and removed en bloc together with the endoscope lens through the endoscopic sheath.

article_title: Case Report: Primary choriocarcinoma of the pineal region
label: Figure 1
caption: Choriocarcinoma of the pineal body in a 9-year-old male patient presenting with dizziness and headache. (a–c) Preoperative CT showed a patchy hyperdense, slightly inhomogeneous lesion in the pineal body with small nodular calcification (white arrow); CT values range from 30 to 119 Hu, and the lesion measures approximately 30 mm × 20 mm × 16 mm. Mild enlargement of the bilateral lateral and third ventricles was noted, along with intraventricular hyperdensity (66 Hu) suggestive of hemorrhage. (d–f) Postoperative CT scans obtained one week later showed complete resection of the lesion, with postoperative changes in the pineal region.

article_title: Falcotentorial dAVF with Unusual Venous Drainage Presenting with Obstructive Hydrocephalus: A Case Report and Literature Review of Endoscopic Third Ventriculostomy Followed by Staged Transarterial Embolization
label: Fig. 1
caption: Heavily T2-weighted CISS MRI demonstrates obstruction of the cerebral aqueduct caused by a dilated draining vein (A). Right VA angiography shows a falcotentorial dAVF supplied by bilateral dural branches of the posterior cerebral arteries and a dural branch of the left superior cerebellar artery, all converging on the shunt point, with associated retrograde venous reflux into the deep venous system; (B) AP view and (C) lateral view. Right CCA angiography demonstrates additional arterial feeders from the posterior convexity branch of the right MMA, the hypoglossal branch of the APA, and the MHT supplying the shunt point; (D) AP view and (E) lateral view. Left CCA angiography further demonstrates arterial supply from the hypoglossal branch of the left; APA and the MHT converging on the same shunt point; (F) AP view and (G) lateral view. Fusion image of MRI CISS and reconstructed DSA MIP at the midbrain level demonstrates the draining vein coursing through the mesencephalon; arteries are shown in red and veins in blue (H).AP, anteroposterior; APA, ascending pharyngeal artery; CCA, common carotid artery; CISS, constructive interference in steady state; dAVF, dural arteriovenous fistula; MHT, meningohypophyseal trunk; MIP, maximum intensity projection; MMA, middle meningeal artery; VA, vertebral artery

article_title: Falcotentorial dAVF with Unusual Venous Drainage Presenting with Obstructive Hydrocephalus: A Case Report and Literature Review of Endoscopic Third Ventriculostomy Followed by Staged Transarterial Embolization
label: Fig. 2
caption: A schematic illustration depicts the venous anatomy of the falcotentorial dAVF. In association with an occluded straight sinus, the draining vein from the shunt point initially descends and connects to the trans-mesencephalic venous pathway, which compresses and obstructs the cerebral aqueduct, resulting in obstructive hydrocephalus. The draining vein then ascends and drains into the vein of Galen, with venous reflux into the ICVs and the BVR. Reflux into the ICVs extends via periventricular veins, causing cortical venous reflux in the left occipital lobe, whereas reflux through the BVR reaches the right DMCV and the uncal vein, and subsequently the left SMCV.BVR, basal vein of Rosenthal; dAVF, dural arteriovenous fistula; DMCV, deep middle cerebral vein; ICV, internal cerebral vein; LMV, lateral mesencephalic vein; SMCV, superficial middle cerebral vein; SPS, superior petrosal sinus

article_title: Falcotentorial dAVF with Unusual Venous Drainage Presenting with Obstructive Hydrocephalus: A Case Report and Literature Review of Endoscopic Third Ventriculostomy Followed by Staged Transarterial Embolization
label: Fig. 3
caption: Endoscopic views obtained during ETV demonstrate an obstructed cerebral aqueduct (arrow) compressed by a dilated trans-mesencephalic venous pathway (arrowhead) (A). An additional endoscopic view shows the anterior surface of the cerebellum and a descending, dilated draining vein (B). A puncture was created at the midpoint between the bilateral mammillary bodies and the infundibular recess, followed by dilation of the puncture site with a balloon catheter (C, D). Preoperative FLAIR MRI demonstrates findings consistent with hydrocephalus (E). Follow-up FLAIR imaging obtained 1 month after ETV shows improvement of hydrocephalus (F).ETV, endoscopic third ventriculostomy

article_title: Falcotentorial dAVF with Unusual Venous Drainage Presenting with Obstructive Hydrocephalus: A Case Report and Literature Review of Endoscopic Third Ventriculostomy Followed by Staged Transarterial Embolization
label: Fig. 4
caption: Preoperative left APA angiography shows arterial supply to the shunt point via the hypoglossal branch (A, B). Left APA angiography after TAE with Onyx via the hypoglossal branch shows the reduction of shunt flow (C, D). Right MMA angiography before the 2nd TAE shows residual arterial supply to the shunt point from the posterior convexity branch of the MMA (E, F). Right external carotid artery angiography after the 2nd TAE shows residual shunt flow with a reduction in shunt volume (G, H). Right VA angiography 1 month after TAE shows the disappearance of the shunt (I, J).APA, ascending pharyngeal artery; MMA, middle meningeal artery; Onyx 18, Medtronic, Irvine, CA, USA; TAE, transarterial embolization; VA, vertebral artery

article_title: A Case of Noncommunicating Hydrocephalus Presenting as Isolated Hyposmia
label: FIGURE 3
caption: Postoperative axial (A) and coronal (B) MRI slices demonstrating reduced ventricular size following endoscopic third ventriculostomy.

article_title: A Case of Noncommunicating Hydrocephalus Presenting as Isolated Hyposmia
label: FIGURE 2
caption: Preoperative axial (A and B) and coronal (C) MRI slices showing symmetrical dilation of the lateral and third ventricles with associated parenchymal thinning, consistent with noncommunicating hydrocephalus.

article_title: 儿童复杂性脑积水的诊治新策略：阶段性手术管理流程
journal: Journal of Central South University Medical Sciences
label: 图1
caption: 儿童复杂性脑积水女婴(22 d)的头颅影像学表现Figure 1 Cranial imaging findings in a 22-day-old female infant with complex hydrocephalusA-C: Preoperative MRI of axial (A), sagittal (B), and coronal planes (C) shows hemorrhage in the bilateral lateral ventricles and third ventricle, and hydrocephalus. D-F: Postoperative CT of axial (D), sagittal (E), and coronal planes (F) shows changes after intraventricular drug pump implantation and lateral ventricular burr hole drainage, with decreased bilateral lateral ventricular hematocele and alleviated hydrocephalus compared with the previous examination.

article_title: 儿童复杂性脑积水的诊治新策略：阶段性手术管理流程
journal: Journal of Central South University Medical Sciences
label: 图2
caption: 儿童复杂性脑积水男婴(1月龄)的头颅影像学表现Figure 2 Cranial imaging findings in a 1-month-old male infant with complex hydrocephalusA-C: Preoperative MRI in the axial (A), sagittal (B), and coronal (C) planes shows old subependymal hemorrhage lesions in both lateral ventricles, ventricular enlargement, and hydrocephalus. D: First postoperative CT in the horizontal plane shows persistent ventricular enlargement and hydrocephalus. E-G: Second postoperative CT in the horizontal (E), sagittal (F), and coronal (G) planes shows post-ventriculoperitoneal shunt changes and progression of supratentorial ventricular system hydrocephalus compared to the previous scan. H-J: Third postoperative CT in the horizontal (H), sagittal (I), and coronal (J) planes shows post-ventriculoperitoneal shunt changes, hydrocephalus with interstitial cerebral edema worsening compared to previous findings. K: Fourth postoperative CT in the horizontal plane shows post-ventriculoperitoneal shunt changes, with supratentorial hydrocephalus markedly worse than previously. L: Fifth postoperative CT in the horizontal plane shows persistent ventricular enlargement.
